title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
SEO Insertions on Effect of BMI on Term Insurance - Article Page
Keyword density and no. of target keywords has been mentioned as comments in the beginning
Suggested additional anchor texts are marked as comments in Yellow. (If required)
Body Content:
Health plays a crucial role in the calculation of your insurance premiums. It's a no-brainer that healthier individuals are less likely to fall ill or develop chronic illnesses that can shorten their lifespan. Hence, your lifestyle, occupation, prior illnesses, and family history all have a direct correlation to your insurance premiums. In fact, your insurer will likely request a complete medical examination for you so they can evaluate your current health status. This is applicable to your BMI as well. But what exactly is BMI?
What is BMI?
BMI is short for Body Mass Index. It is a formula that determines a person’s weight relative to her height to ascertain whether the person is healthy at a broad level. A good BMI indicator takes into account the person’s gender, age, height, and weight to determine their body mass index. A normal BMI score tends to lie anywhere between 18.5 and 24.9, indicating that a person’s weight is proportionate to her height.
To calculate BMI, your body weight is divided by the square of your height. Online BMI calculators will do this for you automatically. One’s body’s mass can therefore be determined by how high the BMI comes out to be. The interpretation of the body mass index in adults is fairly straightforward. One can use a BMI chart to categorise a person into any one of the following classifications:
Normal or ‘healthy’ weight (if BMI falls between 18.5 and 24.9)
Underweight (if BMI is lower than the normal range)
Overweight (if BMI is higher than the normal range)
Obese (if BMI is higher than the overweight range)
Now that we understand the basics of BMI, what is the relationship between BMI and term insurance plans?
Effect of  BMI on Term Insurance Premium
Some insurers assess how high your term insurance plan’s premiums will be based on your BMI. If your BMI falls within the normal range, you will likely be offered the best premium you can get for your age group, provided you qualify as healthy in other domains as well. Assuming your BMI comes out to be in the overweight category, your insurer might issue your policy at a higher premium than what you would get at a normal BMI.
Although this may not be the case for all insurers, it is expected that the higher your BMI (with respect to age and gender), the more your premium may increase. The logic behind this rule of thumb is simple. When an individual has a higher BMI, studies have shown that they are more likely to develop chronic illnesses like heart disease and stroke. But what about those with BMI that classifies them as underweight?
Some studies show that underweight individuals might have vitamin and mineral deficiencies which sets the stage for a host of chronic diseases if these deficiencies are not dealt with. For instance, a vitamin D deficiency can result in bone loss over the years and potentially result in osteoporosis, but the deficiency will need to be chronic for this to happen. Hence, this added risk may result in slightly higher premiums for underweight individuals as well.
Should you Buy a Term Insurance Plan if your BMI is High?
Regardless of your BMI, you should assess how much a term plan can benefit your portfolio and the security of your loved ones when deciding whether or not to buy it. You get the added benefit of a more affordable policy than a traditional life insurance policy since you are insured for a specific duration in term insurance plans. So, even if your premiums are higher due to your BMI, they might still be lower than what you would have to pay for traditional life insurance, depending on the insurer you go for.
For those with higher or lower BMIs, while your premiums might be high, your loved ones will be financially protected through a term plan in the unfortunate case of your loss. But what if your policy matures and you outlive it? Being customizable is another great feature of term plans. If you were to outlive your policy tenure, you can always extend the duration of your term plan. Hence, there are multiple perks to choosing term insurance plans when planning to secure the future of your loved ones.
Conclusion
Term insurance is one of the best ways to safeguard the finances of your loved ones in your absence. While your BMI can affect the premiums that are offered to you when you purchase a term plan, definitely consider all the pros and cons of going for term insurance before you make a decision about adding it to your portfolio.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale.
full_pdf_text:
Effect of BMI on Term Insurance
Health plays a crucial role in the calculation of your insurance premiums. It's a no-brainer that healthier individuals are less likely to fall ill or develop chronic illnesses that can shorten their lifespan. Hence, your lifestyle, occupation, prior illnesses, and family history all have a direct correlation to your insurance premiums. In fact, your insurer is likely to request a complete medical examination for you so they can evaluate your current health status. This is applicable to your BMI as well. But what exactly is BMI?
What is BMI?
BMI is short for Body Mass Index. It is a formula that determines a person’s weight relative to his/her height to ascertain whether the person is healthy at a broad level. A good BMI indicator considers the person’s gender, age, height, and weight to determine their body mass index. A normal BMI score tends to lie anywhere between 18.5 and 24.9, indicating that a person’s weight is proportionate to his/her height. 2
To calculate BMI, your body weight is divided by the square of your height. Online BMI calculators will do this for your automatically. One’s body’s mass can therefore be determined by how high the BMI comes out to be. One can use a BMI chart top categorize a person into any one of the following classifications1:
1. If BMI falls between 18.5 and 24.9, it is considered as normal or healthy weight 2. If BMI is lower than 18.5, it is considered as underweight 3. If BMI is higher than 25 to 29.9 it is considered as overweight 4. If BMI is higher than 30, it is considered as obese.
Now that we understand the basics of BMI, what is the relationship between BMI and term insurance plans?
Effect of BMI on Term Insurance Premium
All insurers assess how high your term insurance plan’s premiums will be based on your BMI. If your BMI falls within the normal range, your premium will remain same and not be increased, provided you qualify as healthy in other domains as well. Assuming your BMI comes out to be in the overweight category, your insurer might issue your policy at a higher premium than what you would get at a normal BMI.
Should you buy a Term Insurance Plan if your BMI is high?
Regardless of your BMI, you should assess how much a term plan can benefit your portfolio and the security of your loved ones when deciding whether or not to buy it.
For those with higher than or lower than standard/normal range of BMI scores, while your premiums might be high, your loved ones will be financially protected through a term plan in the unfortunate
case of your loss Hence, there are multiple benefits of choosing term insurance plans when planning to secure the financial future of your loved ones.
Conclusion
Term insurance is one of the preferred ways to safeguard the finances of your loved ones in your absence. While your BMI can affect the premiums that are offered to you when you purchase a term plan, consider all the pros and cons of going for term insurance before you decide about adding it to your portfolio.
Source:
1https://www.cdc.gov/healthyweight/assessing/bmi/adult_bmi/index.html#:~:text=Body%20mass% 20index%20(BMI)%20is,square%20of%20height%20in%20meters.
2https://www.nhs.uk/common-health-questions/lifestyle/what-is-the-body-mass-index-bmi/
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions.
